frameworks.name: Any-Answer-Except-Call-Me Operator Test
frameworks.definition: A role-play interview used to verify a GM candidate can handle operational crises (upset customer, facility emergency) without escalating to the owner; any response except calling the owner is the right answer.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that for a med spa operator running both direct operations and a consulting business, the consulting-driven rollup (50+ location exit) is the dominant opportunity, and he would accept lower margin on the existing spa locations to free time for the rollup rather than optimize the spa operations directly.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: One business has a clearly higher-value exit path (rollup) than the other
claims.mechanism: Time and attention are the binding constraint; sacrificing margin on lower-value operations to hire a capable operator frees the founder's bandwidth entirely for the higher-value path.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a coaching or consulting business that serves med spa operators can double as the acquisition funnel for a portfolio rollup: coaching clients become the candidate pool from which the 50-100 best operators are selected for a group exit.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Coaching business targets operators in the same vertical as the rollup
Coach has credibility from operating in the same industry
claims.mechanism: Coaching creates pre-qualified, pre-vetted operator relationships before any acquisition conversation, reducing deal-sourcing friction and due-diligence cost for the rollup.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that how frequently a founder physically visits their locations (in this case: zero to twice per week) is the operative test of whether the business is truly outsourced, not whether the founder claims it is.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends compensating a GM-level hire with a base salary (~$100k/year), a 5-10% profit share vesting over four years, and a bonus tied to the contribution of those locations to the overall EBITDA upon exit, as a non-dilutive alternative to equity that aligns the GM with both operations and the eventual sale.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Location-based service business targeting a rollup exit
Founder wants GM skin-in-the-game without transferring cap-table equity
claims.mechanism: Tying compensation to EBITDA contribution at exit aligns the GM's interest with maximizing the exact metric buyers value in a rollup, without diluting the founder's ownership.

anchors.value: 10% of those locations' contribution to overall EBITDA at sale

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the correct framing when hiring a GM for a service-business location is to grant the GM named ownership of the facility in spirit (put their picture on the wall, call it their facility) so they psychologically own outcomes, while the legal title and economics remain structured to benefit the rollup.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder needs GM to operate autonomously
claims.mechanism: Psychological ownership (named facility, pictured on wall) increases GM initiative and crisis-handling willingness beyond what financial incentives alone produce.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that deal structure nuances in a med spa rollup can simultaneously make the deal more tax-efficient for the seller and make it appear more attractive to the acquired operators, creating a win-win that improves deal close rates.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is both acquirer and exit beneficiary
bounded_by: Specific nuances are context-dependent and outside the scope of the conversation

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that for a location-based service business targeting a rollup exit, pursuing the rollup is so disproportionately high-value that virtually nothing else in the business competes with it as a use of the founder's time.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder's operational involvement in existing locations is replaceable
claims.mechanism: The exit multiple on a 50+ location group is orders of magnitude larger than any incremental improvement to daily operations of two existing locations.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a med spa coaching or consulting business that charges operators approximately $6,000 per year can serve as a financially self-sustaining pipeline for identifying and qualifying candidates for a portfolio rollup, effectively generating deal flow at low cost.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Consulting serves same vertical as rollup target
Coaching price (~$6k/year) is sufficient to cover program costs
claims.mechanism: Paid consulting relationships create observation windows into operator quality, creating a pre-qualified acquisition pipeline that conventional deal-sourcing channels cannot replicate.

numbers.axis: Consulting fee per operator per year

examples.text: During the live session, Alex Hormozi role-played the operator test with the med spa founder. He described two crisis scenarios: (1) an upset patient who had an unusual reaction to a body-contouring treatment arriving at the location; (2) the ceiling starting to leak mid-business-day. For each, he told the candidate GM that any answer except calling the owner was the right answer. He used this role-play to illustrate the criterion for a qualified GM: someone who will own operational decisions entirely without escalating to the founder, which is the prerequisite for the founder to pursue the rollup full-time.

The med spa founder described running two businesses simultaneously: (1) two in-person med spa locations generating $1.2M in revenue on track for the year, offering a single service (body contouring); and (2) a consulting program charging other med spa operators approximately $6,000/year to help them scale using the founder's operational pillars. Alex Hormozi used this dual-business structure to identify the rollup path: the coaching clients become the candidate pool for a 50-100 location group exit, making the consulting business the deal-flow engine for the rollup rather than a standalone income source.

practices.text: Before hiring a GM to run a service-business location, role-play operational crises with the candidate and require them to handle each scenario without calling you; only candidates who can do this without hesitation should be hired.

When handing over a facility to a GM, put the GM's picture on the wall and refer to it as their facility, not yours, to create psychological ownership that drives above-and-beyond operational behavior.

For rollup deal structures, consult an advisor on restructuring options before pitching operators, as a few structural changes can simultaneously improve your tax position and make the deal appear more attractive to target operators.
